utterances: For NPR News, I'm Kyle G.
Brown in Johannesburg.
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
This year's campaign for the Democratic presidential nomination can be very personal.
Each of us has to confront our beliefs about sex, race and power.

One week from today, we'll be at Morgan State University in Baltimore to talk about African-Americans' sometimes conflicted feelings about Senator Barack Obama and the prospect of a first black president.
Today, we address the range of responses women talk about when they consider Senator Hillary Clinton and the possibility of a first female president.
We'll talk with some of the contributors to a new book ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary: Reflections by Women Writers"" and we invite women in the audience to call us with their views.
Do you make your decisions of loyalty on the issues?
Does personality play a part?
And how much does Senator Clinton's baggage figure in - the Lewinski affair, the line about baking cookies, the perception that she is aloof and calculating, and her style?
Our phone number is 800-989-8255.
E-mail us talk@npr.org.
And you can join the conversation on our blog at npr.org/blogofthenation.
We begin with Susan Morrison, articles editor at the New Yorker magazine and editor of ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary."" She joins us from our bureau in New York.
And it's nice to have you on the program today.
Thank you.
Nice to be here.
And as women look at this choice, why isn't it simple?
Well, there are rather more than 30 ways, I can tell you.
Huh.

Sometime last spring, I started realizing that every time a conversation that I was in got around to Hillary Clinton, suddenly voices were raised, faces got red, people just got much more exercised about her.
People have conversations about her that they would never have about Mitt Romney or John Edwards.
And, you know, I have realized that this was particularly true among women.

Hillary herself has said over the years that she is a Rorschach test and so I just decided to check that concept - Hillary as a Rorschach test - and basically, you know, ask 30 women to, as it were, submit themselves to the Hillary Rorschach test and write about what kinds of buttons they push - she pushes in them, where the reactions toward her come from.
And the results were really quite interesting.

A number of the women started out being what Nora Ephron has memorably called Hillary resistors - people who wanted to like Hillary but for one reason or another, felt that they were having trouble liking her.

And it was just really interesting to see these women had put their reactions to her under the microscope, poke at them, prod them and, you know, figure out which ones are valid and which ones are not.
In your introduction you write: Where one person sees a carpetbagger, another sees a deft politician.
Where one sees a humiliated and long-suffering wife, another sees a dignified first lady.
So a lot of this depends on where you're standing when you're looking.
Right.
At first, I thought that some of the passion about Hillary might have to do with the fact that we had already lived with her in the public eye for so many years.

That she'd already lived in the White House for eight years, we knew more about her than we should because of the Starr Report and all kinds of other, you know, other things that went on during the Clinton administration.
But I think it's more than that.
I think it's because she's a woman.
And so many women, at least in my generation and the baby boomer generation, identified with her for so long and particularly at the beginning.
Thank you.
Absolutely.
That's the thing that's so hilarious about all this is cloud computing.
The Internet is cloud computing.
Now if the White House is going to use this kind of computing, the next question has to be - is it secure?
One aspect of cloud computing is actually knowing where information is.
This is government contractor and computer security expert, Kevin Jackson.
If you don't know where the information is, how can you prove that it's being protected?
Right, so it seems to make sense to me, if you have a computer, and you have to protect what's in it, that you just secure that computer really, really well.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Right.
Absolutely.
With a cloud, what do you do?
Right.
One of the aspects of it is actually proving that information has not been changed by a bad actor.
It seems like levels of access would be another question.
Access control.
Right.
Managing who can access it, when they can access it.
And it's not just a question of who can get a hold of sensitive government information, but whether the government can get a hold of your sensitive information.
Then again, Jackson says in some ways, cloud computing is more secure.
If you think about it, let's say all your information is on a sheet of paper.

If you take that sheet of paper and encrypt it, and put it in one location, that's typically how things have been done in the past, but in the cloud, if you take that sheet of paper, encrypt it, then tear it up into four or five pieces, and then put those pieces in five different locations around the world, now even if someone got one of those pieces, they wouldn't have all your information.
Are you saying that cloud computing could actually be safer?
Yes, in a lot of ways it can be safer, because there's no physical location for someone to attack.

In fact, if you look at Amazon or Google, a piece of information actually exists in multiple locations around the world, so it would be difficult to even identify where that information is, let alone go after it.
There are other distinct advantages to cloud computing.
It makes collaboration on projects vastly easier, and that's something the government is notoriously bad at.
Many people can work on one single document, instead of saving 12 different versions of it on 12 different laptops.
Vint Cerf is the scientist known as the Father of the Internet, and he's now at Google.
He says there's another advantage.

If this administration evolves in the way that it appears to be evolving, it's likely to be very substantial and increasing interest on the part of the public in engaging, and seeing what the administration is doing.
So, the cloud is just really a good paradigm for achieving that objective.
So if all the tech gurus - Vint Cerf, Microsoft's Steve Ballmer, the people at Google, think the White House should use cloud computing, is there really any choice?
There is.
Up to now, internal White House computer networks have been kept behind heavy firewalls.
The new approach would mean a rethinking of all the systems and regulations now in place.
And if the transition team isn't even sure if Obama can have a BlackBerry?
Switching the entire White House to cloud computing could be more change than they want to take on.
New York's highest court is considering whether gays and lesbians have the right to marry.
Yesterday, the justices heard arguments from advocates representing more than 40 same-sex couples.
They were denied marriage licenses.
If the court rules in their favor, New York could move closer to being the second state to allow same-sex marriages.
NPR's Robert Smith reports.
It was more than two years ago that Joanne Shane(ph) and Mary Joe Kennedy(ph) dressed for their wedding day.
The two women showed up at the New York City Clerk's Office, plopped down their money for a marriage license and were politely turned down.
Unidentified Woman #1: We brought our cameras and took pictures of it.
Unidentified Woman #2: And afterwards, actually, I sent out announcements via e-mail to my various friends.
Instead of a wedding announcement it was a denial of wedding announcements, you know.
Now, the New York State Court of Appeals will finally decide if that denial was Constitutional.
Outside the Albany courthouse yesterday afternoon, same-sex couples from around the state, who also had been refused licenses, lined up to attend the oral arguments.
The Court had consolidated four separate lawsuits.
Unidentified Woman #1: We're actually, today, introducing ourselves to the couples from other cases that we don't know.
Unidentified Woman #2: We've never met them before, and it's nice to meet some of the other plaintiffs.
(Soundbite of court proceedings)
Hear ye, hear ye, hear ye.
Inside the courtroom, the justices were using the case to explore the history of marriage from, as one judge put it, the dawn of civilization to the very recent debate over no fault divorces.

Advocates for the same sex couples tried to make a very simple case that the state's 97-year old marriage law, which refers to husband and wife, doesn't live up to modern standards of equal protection under the law.
Terence Kindlon is one of the lawyers for the same sex couples.
We are evolving as a society.
And just because we did something 200 years ago or 100 years ago or 50 years ago, it's not necessarily a good justification to do it now.
